region_of_origin: Central and South America (widely cultivated across Asia and globally)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw red chilli is approximately 1 medium pod (about 15g), commonly used as a condiment or flavouring ingredient in Southeast Asian and South Asian cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli, red, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for 'Chilli, red, raw' (CR ID: F14040043). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11819 (raw red chilli pepper) as 0.04g/100g. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and select micronutrients not reported by HPB; supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11819 where available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw red chilli is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 23 is estimated based on the closest analogous entry for fresh capsicum/pepper (Capsicum annuum) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The very low carbohydrate content (4.2g/100g) and very high dietary fibre (10.6g/100g) support a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (23 × 0.63) / 100 ≈ 0.1 (based on 15g serving containing approximately 0.63g available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3mg/100g) and high potassium (300mg/100g) with very high dietary fibre (10.6g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Raw red chilli is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium content (3mg/100g), high potassium-to-sodium ratio, and very high dietary fibre align strongly with DASH dietary guidelines for blood pressure management — however, it is typically consumed in small quantities as a condiment, so ensure accompanying dishes remain low in sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.2g/100g), very high dietary fibre (10.6g/100g), and capsaicin-mediated insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw red chilli is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes; its low net carbohydrate content, very high fibre-to-sugar ratio, and bioactive capsaicin compound (which has been shown in peer-reviewed studies to support insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism) result in a negligible glycaemic impact at typical serving sizes — avoid sweet chilli sauces which carry a substantially different nutritional profile with added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.04g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre, and capsaicin with antioxidant and vasodilatory properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw red chilli is suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre, and the vasodilatory and anti-inflammatory properties of capsaicin support cardiovascular health — consume as a whole-food spice rather than in processed chilli-based sauces that may contain high sodium or added fats.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable (Capsicum genus) containing solanine-related alkaloids that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw red chilli is classified as a nightshade vegetable and is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who have confirmed nightshade sensitivity, as solanine-related alkaloids may aggravate joint inflammation — individuals should monitor personal response and substitute with non-nightshade anti-inflammatory spices such as ginger or turmeric if sensitivity is confirmed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw red chilli (Capsicum annuum) is a fresh, uncooked hot pepper widely used as a spice and condiment across Southeast Asian, South Asian, East Asian, and global cuisines. The pods are firm, bright red, and deliver a characteristic hot, pungent, and slightly fruity flavour driven by the bioactive compound capsaicin. In Singapore and the broader Southeast Asian region, raw red chilli is a foundational ingredient in sambal, chilli pastes, dipping sauces, and stir-fries. It is consumed in small quantities — typically 10–20g per serving — as a flavouring agent rather than a primary food. Nutritionally, raw red chilli is remarkably dense for its caloric content: at only 48 kcal per 100g, it provides 1.4g protein, 0.4g fat, 4.2g carbohydrate, and an exceptionally high 10.6g dietary fibre per 100g. The fibre content is notably high for a non-leafy vegetable and contributes to its very low glycaemic index (estimated GI ~23). Sodium is negligible at 3mg/100g while potassium is substantial at 300mg/100g, making it highly compatible with DASH dietary guidelines for hypertension management. Capsaicin has been studied for its roles in supporting insulin sensitivity, vasodilation, anti-inflammatory pathways, and thermogenesis. Raw red chilli is also a rich source of vitamin C (~144mg/100g), vitamin B6, and provitamin A. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. As a nightshade vegetable, it warrants caution for individuals with confirmed rheumatoid arthritis nightshade sensitivity. It should not be confused with sweet chilli sauce or dried chilli powder, which have substantially different nutritional profiles.
common_user_queries: Is red chilli good for diabetes?
How many calories in red chilli?
Is red chilli high in fibre?
Can I eat red chilli if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of red chilli?
Nutritional value of raw red chilli per 100g
Is red chilli good for weight loss?
Does capsaicin help with blood sugar?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical primary source for Singapore food records, CR ID F14040043, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and select micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11819 (raw red chilli pepper), an approved secondary source. GI estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database for fresh Capsicum annuum, with estimation basis fully documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.04g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.02g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.21g/100g), trans fat (0.0g/100g), and select micronutrients (vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this record and have been supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11819 (Peppers, hot chili, red, raw) as an approved secondary source. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0g/100g — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central NDB 11819. Vitamin B12 is 0.0g/100g — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. Vitamin D is 0.0mcg/100g — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for raw red chilli. Cholesterol is 0.0mg/100g — naturally zero as this is a plant food. 3. GI of 23 is an analogous estimate based on fresh capsicum/pepper data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for raw red chilli is available in the database as of the accessed date. 4. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' present in the input record has been removed as it is not applicable to chilli pepper. The tag 'keto' has been retained as a dietary flag given the very low net carbohydrate content. 5. This food is a nightshade vegetable (Capsicum genus); individuals with confirmed rheumatoid arthritis nightshade sensitivity should exercise caution. 6. No Chinese-script local name has been assigned as this is a generic raw ingredient record without a specific Singaporean dish identity requiring mandatory Chinese naming under G6/Q1 rules — the food is a global raw ingredient, not a Singaporean dish. Malay (Lada merah / Cili merah), Tamil (சிவப்பு மிளகாய்), and Hindi (लाல मिर्च) aliases are provided for regional completeness.
